food-01: This beautiful 1875 storefront, with its painted glass ceiling and gilded lettering, is a listed historic monument — and baker Christophe Vasseur left a career in fashion to save it. His pistachio-chocolate 'escargot' pastry is now world-famous, but his pain des amis, a slow-fermented bread with a smoky crust, is what made chefs across Paris order from him. The bakery still closes every weekend, just like an old village boulangerie.
food-02: Pierre Hermé was born into four generations of Alsatian bakers and apprenticed at fourteen under the legendary Gaston Lenôtre. Vogue magazine crowned him 'the Picasso of pastry' for treating flavors like an artist treats color — his rose, lychee and raspberry Ispahan was mocked when he invented it, and is now one of the most copied desserts on Earth. He even releases his macarons in seasonal 'collections,' exactly like a fashion house.
food-03: This is the oldest patisserie in Paris, opened in 1730 by Nicolas Stohrer, who came from Poland as the personal pastry chef of Queen Marie Leszczyńska, wife of Louis XV. It was here that he invented the baba au rhum — legend says the queen's father found his brioche too dry and asked for it soaked in sweet wine. The painted ceiling and mirrored interior were decorated by Paul Baudry, the same artist who painted the foyer of the Opéra Garnier.
food-04: Pierre Poilâne opened his bakery in 1932, insisting on stone-ground flour, sea salt from Guérande and a wood-fired oven when industrial bread was taking over Paris. His granddaughter Apollonia took over at just eighteen, while still a Harvard student, after her parents died in a helicopter crash — she ran the bakery between classes, from her dorm room. Salvador Dalí once commissioned Poilâne to build him a bedroom entirely out of bread.
food-05: Angelina opened in 1903 and was named after the founder's daughter-in-law; Coco Chanel came here almost daily for the hot chocolate, always sitting at table number ten. The recipe for 'L'Africain' — made from three secret African cocoas — hasn't changed in over a century. Marcel Proust, who lived around the corner, was also a regular under these Belle Époque mirrors.
food-06: Raymond Berthillon was an innkeeper's son who started churning ice cream in the back of his mother-in-law's café on Île Saint-Louis in 1954. A food critic discovered him in 1961 and wrote that the best ice cream in the world was hiding on a little island in the Seine — the line outside has never left. The family still makes everything on the island, and famously closes in part of summer, because in their view even ice cream makers deserve a holiday.
food-07: Rue des Rosiers has been the heart of Jewish Paris since the Middle Ages, and L'As du Fallafel — 'the ace of falafel' — has been its noisy, joyful king since 1979. Lenny Kravitz once called it the best falafel in the world, and the quote is still painted proudly on the storefront. The street's name means 'street of the rosebushes,' a memory of the gardens that grew here seven hundred years ago.
food-08: Bertrand Larcher did something deliciously backwards: he opened his first crêperie in Tokyo, then brought the Japanese obsession with perfect ingredients back to Brittany and Paris. His galettes use organic buckwheat and butter from Jean-Yves Bordier, who hand-paddles each block like it's sculpture. Food critics credit this little restaurant with single-handedly elevating the humble crêpe from street snack to gastronomy in Paris.
food-09: This is the oldest covered market in Paris, opened in 1628 under Louis XIII. Its name — 'market of the red children' — remembers the orphanage that stood here, whose children wore red uniforms, the color of charity. It nearly became a parking garage in the 1990s until the neighborhood fought to save it; today it's the lunchtime living room of the Marais.
food-10: Alain Roussel — 'Alain la Crêpe' — started with a crêpe stand and became a Marais legend by refusing to hurry: every sandwich is built with the ceremony of a tasting menu, with organic bread he grills himself and cheese he shaves in front of you. The line can take an hour, and Alain will happily chat with you for part of it. His philosophy, printed on the wall: slow food, made with love, eaten with your hands.
food-11: Bertrand Auboyneau's bistro is the standard-bearer of 'bistronomie' — the movement that put serious cooking back into casual Paris bistros. The dining room is a museum of authentic 1930s fixtures he salvaged from closing cafés across France. Anthony Bourdain came here for the steak au poivre and called the Paris-Brest — a choux pastry invented in 1910 to celebrate a bicycle race — one of the best desserts of his life.
food-12: Tucked behind the Place des Vosges, Chez Janou pours more pastis than almost anywhere north of Marseille — over eighty varieties of the anise-scented spirit of Provence. The help-yourself chocolate mousse arrives in a bowl the size of a sink, a tradition that started as a kitchen joke and became the most photographed dessert in the Marais. The bistro itself dates to the 1900s and still has its original zinc bar.
food-13: Bouillon Chartier opened in 1896 with a radical idea: feed workers a proper French meal at a price they could afford, in a room as grand as any bourgeois restaurant. It has served an estimated fifty million meals, and waiters still tally your bill in pencil directly on the paper tablecloth. The word 'bouillon' comes from the beef broth these workers' canteens originally served — and the soaring Belle Époque hall is now a listed monument.
food-14: Café de Flore opened in the 1880s and became the office of existentialism: Sartre and Simone de Beauvoir wrote here every day during the war years, partly because the café had heating and their apartments didn't. Sartre said: 'The road to Flore was, for me, the road to freedom.' Picasso, Camus and Hemingway were regulars too, and the café still awards its own literary prize every year.
food-15: Holybelly was opened in 2013 by Nico Alary and Sarah Mouchot, who learned their trade in the coffee shops of Melbourne and brought the anglo brunch revolution to Paris. Their motto, printed on every receipt: 'It's good because we care.' The pancake stack with bacon and bourbon butter is so beloved that regulars revolted when it briefly left the menu — it came back.
food-16: This standing-room cave à vin is the casual little sister of Septime, the restaurant a few doors down that's regularly voted among the World's 50 Best. It pours 'natural' wines — made with wild yeasts and nothing added — from cult producers whose bottles sell out the morning they arrive. The natural wine movement that conquered the world's hip restaurants was born in Paris bars exactly like this one.
food-17: Camille Fourmont ran the bar at a Michelin-starred restaurant before opening this tiny former crémerie — the old dairy-shop tiles and marble are still in place. Her giant white beans with lemon zest became so iconic that the New York Times wrote about them, and her book 'Drinking French' carried this little room's spirit around the world. Twenty seats, candlelight, and one of the best natural wine lists in Paris.
food-18: Laurent Dubois is a Meilleur Ouvrier de France — the craftsman title so prestigious that holders wear a tricolor collar, and the competition to win it has driven chefs to tears on national television. He ages his cheeses in his own cellars beneath Paris, layering Roquefort with quince or truffling Brie like a perfumer composing a scent. France produces over a thousand cheeses, and this shop is one of the best places on Earth to meet them.
food-19: Jacques Genin calls himself a 'fondeur en chocolat' — a chocolate smelter — and taught himself the craft after starting work in kitchens at thirteen. For years his caramels were a secret supplied only to Paris's grandest hotels and restaurants; chefs whispered his name like a password. The mango-passion fruit caramel, soft as silk and sharp as fruit, is widely considered the best caramel made anywhere in the world.
food-20: Chef Stéphane Jégo spent eight years under the great Yves Camdeborde, godfather of the bistronomie movement, before taking over this rowdy rugby-loving Basque bistro in 2002. His riz au lait — rice pudding whipped with salted caramel and hazelnut praline — is so famous that tables order it before they've seen the menu. The room is loud, elbow-to-elbow, and exactly what the French mean when they say 'convivialité.'
local-01: This canal was ordered by Napoleon in 1802 to bring clean drinking water to a city ravaged by cholera — and paid for with a new tax on wine, which Parisians found very on-brand. Its iron footbridges and nine locks starred in the film Amélie, where she skipped stones here. In summer, the whole of young Paris sits along these quays at dusk with wine and crisps — the cheapest perfect evening in the city.
local-02: When this railway viaduct fell silent, Paris turned it into the world's first elevated park in 1993 — New York's High Line copied it fifteen years later, and its designers said so openly. You walk three storeys above the street through tunnels of roses and bamboo, on the exact route trains once took to Bastille station, which is now the opera house. Under the arches below, the Viaduc des Arts houses craftsmen's workshops.
local-03: These cliffs, waterfalls and grottos are entirely artificial — Napoleon III built the park in 1867 on top of abandoned gypsum quarries and one of the city's grimmest spots, a former gibbet where bodies of the executed were displayed. The romantic temple on the cliff is a copy of the Temple of Vesta at Tivoli. Parisians say it has the best sunset lawns in the city, and they're right.
local-04: There has been a market on this spot since 1781, and it remains the cheapest, loudest, most Parisian market in the city — vendors here still shout their prices like a sport. The covered Beauvau hall survived the Revolution, and the little flea market alongside has been dealing in curiosities for two centuries. The bar around the corner, Le Baron Rouge, serves oysters and wine straight from the barrel on Sunday mornings — join the crowd standing around the wine casks on the pavement.
local-05: Belleville was its own rebellious village until Paris swallowed it in 1860, and it never quite surrendered — this is where the Paris Commune made its last stand in 1871. Édith Piaf was born on its streets, allegedly under a lamppost on the rue de Belleville. Today it's the city's most exciting mix: Chinese noodle shops, North African cafés, artist studios, and on Rue Dénoyez, walls that get repainted by street artists almost weekly.
local-06: This little street of pastel cottages was built in 1865 as cheap housing for workers, each house identical until residents painted them like a box of macarons. It's been a film set, a fashion-shoot favorite, and an Instagram phenomenon so intense the residents once campaigned to gate it at weekends. Come early, whisper, and it feels like a village street that floated in from the seaside.
local-07: Opened in 1799, this is the oldest covered passage in Paris, built when a hundred glass-roofed arcades let Parisians shop out of the mud and rain — Walter Benjamin called them 'the original temples of commodity capitalism.' The engraver Stern, printer to kings and presidents, worked at number 47 for nearly two centuries. Today its stamp dealers, old print shops and bistros make it feel like the 19th century never quite ended.
local-08: The original Shakespeare and Company, run by Sylvia Beach, published James Joyce's Ulysses in 1922 when no one else would touch it. George Whitman opened this successor in 1951 and let over thirty thousand writers — he called them 'tumbleweeds' — sleep among the shelves in exchange for a few hours of work and a promise to read a book a day. His motto is painted above a doorway: 'Be not inhospitable to strangers lest they be angels in disguise.'
local-09: This rolling English-style park opened in 1869 on the site of old quarries and a famous cabaret. On its opening day, legend says, the lake mysteriously emptied overnight — the engineers had cracked the basin — and Haussmann reportedly hid the embarrassment until it was fixed. The meridian line of Paris runs through the park, marked by a stone obelisk, and the surrounding villa streets hide some of the loveliest artist houses in the city.
local-10: This was Gare Ornano, a station on the Petite Ceinture railway that circled Paris from 1852 until cars killed it in the 1930s. Reborn as an eco-café and urban farm, it keeps chickens where commuters once waited, and its terrace spills directly onto the old tracks. The name is a pun: 'recyclerie' as in recycling — of objects, of buildings, of a whole forgotten railway.
local-11: Behind an unmarked gate on the canal hides what its founders call a 'ghetto museum' — a sprawling cabinet of curiosities celebrating African and Caribbean culture, filled with colonial-era oddities, taxidermy, and vintage rum bottles. It was once a warehouse and stable; now it hosts DJs, secret concerts and one of the best rum selections in Paris. Finding the entrance is half the initiation.
local-12: This hilltop village escaped Haussmann's demolitions because the ground beneath it — riddled with old quarries — couldn't support his heavy stone buildings. Its name means 'hill of the quails,' and its low houses and cobbled lanes still feel like pre-industrial Paris. The Art Nouveau swimming pool on Place Paul Verlaine is fed by a natural artesian well drilled in 1866, and locals still fill bottles at its fountain.
local-13: The flea market was born here in the 1880s when rag-pickers — 'chiffonniers' — were pushed out of Paris and set up just beyond the city walls. It grew into the largest antiques market in the world: seven hectares, a dozen sub-markets, and dealers who have furnished Hollywood films and presidential palaces. The word 'flea market' itself — marché aux puces — was coined here, supposedly for the fleas in the second-hand mattresses.
local-14: The Petite Ceinture — 'little belt' — was a steam railway that circled all of Paris from 1852, carrying passengers and freight between the city's main stations. The Métro killed it by 1934, and for ninety years nature has been quietly reclaiming the tracks: foxes, bats and two hundred plant species now live along the line. Walking between the rails here is the closest Paris gets to urban time travel.
local-15: The Marais was the aristocratic quarter of Paris until the Revolution sent its mansions into centuries of decline — which is exactly what preserved them. The vintage shops of Rue de la Verrerie occupy the ground floors of buildings older than the United States. Parisian fashion students treat Free'P'Star's one-euro bins as a rite of passage: legend has it that genuine Hermès scarves have surfaced from them.
local-16: This little house was built for the lock-keeper who operated the canal locks of the Bassin de la Villette, the artificial lake Napoleon created in 1808 as a reservoir for his new canals. The basin was the industrial port of Paris for a century — flour, wine and timber all landed here. Now the lock-keeper's cottage serves coffee and brunch, and you can sit in a bathtub upstairs with your laptop, which the owners insist is exactly what the lock-keeper would have wanted.
tour-01: Believe it or not, the tower was supposed to be torn down after just twenty years — Parisians called it a 'metal asparagus' and artists signed petitions against it when it opened for the 1889 World's Fair. It survived only because Gustave Eiffel cleverly turned it into a giant radio antenna, which later intercepted enemy messages during World War One. And here's a fun one: on a hot summer day, the iron expands and the tower actually grows about six inches taller.
tour-02: Before it was the world's most visited museum, the Louvre was a medieval fortress — and on the lower level you can still touch the original 12th-century castle walls. The Mona Lisa only became world-famous after an Italian handyman stole her in 1911 and hid her in his apartment for two years. The glass pyramid was hugely controversial in 1989, but today it's as beloved as the palace itself.
tour-03: This cathedral took nearly two hundred years to build, starting in 1163, and it's seen everything — Napoleon crowned himself emperor right here in 1804. When fire tore through the roof in April 2019, the whole world watched the spire fall, but firefighters formed a human chain to save the relics, and incredibly, the bees living in hives on the roof survived. After a five-year restoration using medieval techniques, Notre-Dame reopened in December 2024, its limestone glowing brighter than it has in centuries.
tour-04: This gleaming white basilica was built as an act of national penance after France's crushing defeat in 1870, on the highest natural point in Paris. Its secret: the stone is a special travertine that releases calcite when it rains, so the basilica actually washes itself whiter with every storm. Inside, the golden mosaic of Christ is one of the largest in the world, and someone has been praying here continuously, day and night, since 1885.
tour-05: Napoleon ordered this arch in 1806 to honor his army, but he never saw it finished — it took thirty years, and he only passed under it in his funeral procession. Beneath your feet lies the Tomb of the Unknown Soldier, where a flame has been rekindled every single evening at 6:30 since 1923, without missing a day — even during Nazi occupation. And in 1919, a daredevil pilot named Charles Godefroy actually flew his biplane straight through the arch.
tour-06: You're standing in what was once a glamorous train station, built for the 1900 World's Fair — those giant golden clocks ran the railway timetable. By 1939 its platforms were too short for modern trains, and it nearly became a parking lot before being reborn in 1986 as the home of the world's greatest Impressionist collection. The lovely irony: the museum now celebrates painters who were rejected and ridiculed by the official art world just across the river.
tour-07: King Louis IX built this chapel in just seven years — lightning speed for the Middle Ages — as a giant jewel box for what he believed was Christ's actual Crown of Thorns, which cost him more than the entire building. Upstairs you're surrounded by fifteen towering windows holding over 1,100 stained-glass scenes telling the Bible from Genesis to the Apocalypse. About two-thirds of that glass is still the original from the 1240s — you're looking at the same light medieval kings saw.
tour-08: This opera house is so extravagant that when Empress Eugénie asked what style it was, architect Charles Garnier replied: 'It's Napoleon III style, Madame!' Beneath the building lies a real water reservoir — the famous 'underground lake' that inspired The Phantom of the Opera, and firefighters still train in it today. And in 1896 a chandelier counterweight really did crash into the audience, the very event that sparked the Phantom legend.
tour-09: Cruising the Seine puts you on a stage where every bridge has a story — the Pont Neuf, the 'New Bridge,' is ironically the oldest one in Paris, finished in 1607. The boats are called Bateaux Mouches — 'fly boats' — not after insects but after the Mouche district of Lyon where they were first built. The entire riverbank you glide past is a UNESCO World Heritage Site: a museum you can float through.
tour-10: Louis XIV transformed his father's modest hunting lodge into the most extravagant palace on Earth, partly to keep his nobles too busy with parties and etiquette to plot against him — at its height, some ten thousand people lived and worked here. The Hall of Mirrors was an outrageous flex: mirrors were luxury items, and France poached Venetian glassmakers to make 357 of them. The gardens are so vast that the fountains couldn't all run at once — engineers turned them on one by one just ahead of the king as he strolled.
tour-11: Sixty-five feet beneath your shoes lie the bones of around six million Parisians — more than twice the city's living population today. In the late 1700s the overflowing cemeteries became a public health nightmare, so for decades priests led nightly processions of bone-filled carts down into these old limestone quarries. The workers arranged skulls and femurs into eerie decorative walls, beneath a doorway that warns: 'Stop! This is the empire of death.'
tour-12: When this inside-out building opened in 1977, critics were horrified — all the pipes and ducts are on the outside, color-coded: blue for air, green for water, yellow for electricity, red for movement. The architects, Renzo Piano and Richard Rogers, were unknowns in their thirties who beat nearly 700 entries, and their 'oil refinery' became one of the most influential buildings of the century. It's closed for renovation until around 2030, but the radical silhouette is still worth admiring from the square.
tour-13: These gardens were created in the early 1600s for Marie de' Medici, a homesick Italian queen who wanted a palace reminiscent of her childhood Florence — her palace is now home to the French Senate. Children have sailed little wooden boats on the central pond since the 1880s, and you can still rent one today. Hidden among the trees: an orchard of hundreds of heirloom apple varieties and a beekeeping school teaching here since 1856.
tour-14: This hilltop village was once cheap, wine-soaked, and outside the city limits — which is exactly why young unknowns named Picasso, Van Gogh, and Renoir could afford to live and paint here. Picasso and his crew shared a ramshackle studio nearby called the Bateau-Lavoir, where he painted the canvas that launched Cubism. The square's portrait artists work licensed spots so coveted the waiting list runs years — and yes, there's still a working vineyard on the back of the hill.
tour-15: The name means 'Elysian Fields' — the paradise of Greek mythology — and it began in 1667 as a tree-lined extension of the Tuileries gardens through open fields. This is where the Tour de France sprints to its finish, and where the city floods on Bastille Day for Europe's largest military parade. The rents here are among the highest on Earth, yet the avenue still hosts the world's biggest New Year's Eve street party.
tour-16: This was meant to be a church — Louis XV promised it to Saint Geneviève if he recovered from illness — but the Revolution converted it into a temple for France's greatest minds. Voltaire, Victor Hugo, Marie Curie and Alexandre Dumas rest in the crypt; Curie's remains are still slightly radioactive. Look up in the nave: this is where Foucault hung his famous pendulum in 1851 and visibly proved the Earth rotates — a replica still swings today.
tour-17: You're standing on the single best view of the Eiffel Tower in Paris — which is exactly why Hitler posed for his infamous photo right here during his only visit to the city in 1940. The palace behind you was built for the 1937 World's Fair, an expo so tense that the Nazi German and Soviet pavilions glared at each other directly across these gardens. The fountains below fire twenty water cannons that become a sparkling show on summer evenings.
tour-18: This riverside fortress was a royal palace before it became the most feared prison of the French Revolution — around 2,700 people were held here before the guillotine. Its most famous prisoner was Marie Antoinette, who spent her final ten weeks in a small cell you can still visit. Look up at the corner tower outside: that clock has been telling Parisians the time since 1370 — the oldest public clock in the city, still ticking.
arts-01: Claude Monet designed these two oval rooms himself, donating his enormous Water Lilies as a gift of peace to France the day after the World War One armistice. He insisted on natural light from above and curved walls so the panels wrap around you — he called it a refuge for 'nerves strained by work.' Monet worked on them nearly blind from cataracts, and they were installed in 1927, just months after his death — he never saw them hung.
arts-02: This elegant mansion was once a run-down boarding house where artists rented cheap rooms — Rodin moved in alongside tenants like the young poet Rilke and dancer Isadora Duncan. When the state wanted to evict everyone, Rodin struck one of art history's great bargains: he donated his entire life's work to France in exchange for living here until his death. The Thinker now broods in a rose garden, surrounded by works by Camille Claudel, his brilliant and tragic student and lover.
arts-03: This grand mansion is nicknamed the Hôtel Salé — the 'salted house' — because it was built in the 1650s for a man who got rich collecting the salt tax. The world's largest Picasso collection landed here through a quirk of French law: when Picasso died in 1973, his heirs paid the inheritance tax in artworks instead of cash. That's why the museum owns the pieces Picasso could never bring himself to sell — the favorites he kept by his side his whole life.
arts-04: This was a 19th-century iron foundry that cast parts for the railways and the French navy before falling silent in the 1990s. Today its raw industrial walls become the canvas: 140 projectors and dozens of speakers dissolve the building into floor-to-ceiling moving paintings, so you literally walk inside a Klimt or a Van Gogh. When it opened in 2018 it was Paris's first fully immersive digital art center, and it drew over a million visitors in its first year.
arts-05: Frank Gehry said he wanted to design 'a magnificent vessel symbolizing the cultural calling of France' — and the result is this glass ship seemingly caught mid-sail in the Bois de Boulogne. Its twelve billowing 'sails' hold 3,600 curved glass panels, every single one unique, requiring software originally developed for fighter jets to engineer. Clever legal detail: because it sits on city parkland, the building is promised to the city of Paris after about fifty years.
arts-06: Built for the 1937 World's Fair, this is now the largest center for contemporary art in Europe — and proudly the strangest, with raw concrete interiors deliberately left unfinished, like a permanent work in progress. It keeps artist-friendly hours, staying open until late, because the founders believed art should run on nightlife time. The name has nothing to do with Japan — it comes from the avenue out front, once called Avenue de Tokio.
arts-07: You're at the oldest building in Montmartre, a 17th-century manor that became a hive of bohemian life — Renoir rented a studio here and painted his luminous Bal du Moulin de la Galette while living on this very property. Later, Suzanne Valadon worked here with her son Maurice Utrillo; she was a former circus acrobat and artists' model who taught herself to paint and became the first woman admitted to France's national fine arts society. From the gardens, a postcard view of Montmartre's own little vineyard.
arts-08: This round building has had many lives: it stands where Marie de' Medici built a palace, became the city's grain exchange in the 1760s, then the commodities trading floor — under one of the first great iron-and-glass domes in the world. In 2021, billionaire collector François Pinault unveiled its boldest chapter: Tadao Ando inserted a vast, perfectly smooth concrete cylinder right inside the historic rotunda — a building within a building. Look up at the dome's frescoes celebrating world trade, then down at some of the most provocative contemporary art anywhere.
arts-09: This intimate mansion holds the world's largest collection of Monets, mostly donated by the painter's own son — including the canvas that named an entire movement: 'Impression, Sunrise,' the hazy harbor view a mocking critic used to coin the word 'Impressionism.' That painting has lived a thriller plot: in 1985, armed robbers stole it in broad daylight, and it vanished for five years before police found it in a villa in Corsica. The museum also keeps a stunning trove of Berthe Morisot, the great woman of Impressionism.
arts-10: In 1999, three artists broke into this abandoned bank building owned by the state and simply started making art — the squat became one of the most visited contemporary art spaces in Paris. Instead of evicting them, the city eventually bought the building and legalized it, a rare happy ending for an artist squat. Today around thirty artists work in open studios across six floors, the spiral staircase is repainted constantly, and you can wander in for free and watch art being made in real time.
exp-01: When Gustave Eiffel built his tower for the 1889 World's Fair, critics called it a monstrosity and it was only meant to stand for twenty years — radio antennas saved it from demolition. The sparkle is surprisingly young, though: those 20,000 golden bulbs were installed for the millennium in 2000, and they shimmer for five minutes at the top of every hour after dark. Fun secret — the light show is actually copyrighted as an artwork, so technically your nighttime photos are for personal use only.
exp-02: You're standing on the very tip of the Île de la Cité, the little island where Paris was born over two thousand years ago. This leafy point is named for King Henri IV, whose nickname was the 'Vert-Galant' — the 'old charmer' — because he was still wooing mistresses well into his fifties; his bronze statue rides above you on the Pont Neuf. A darker tale: near this spot in 1314, Jacques de Molay, the last Grand Master of the Knights Templar, was burned at the stake — legend says he cursed the king with his dying breath.
exp-03: Surprise — the croissant isn't originally French at all! It descends from the Austrian kipferl, brought to Paris by a Viennese baker in the 1830s, and French pâtissiers transformed it with laminated butter dough into the flaky marvel we know. In this riverside kitchen you'll learn the real secret: dozens of paper-thin butter layers folded with sacred patience — and in France, a croissant made with real butter is traditionally straight, while the curved ones signal margarine.
exp-04: The macaron came to France in the 1500s with Catherine de' Medici's Italian pastry chefs, but for centuries it was just a humble almond cookie — the genius move of sandwiching two shells around ganache happened in Paris around 1930, at Ladurée. You'll learn the craft from chefs of Ferrandi, the school nicknamed 'the Harvard of gastronomy,' under Galeries Lafayette's stained-glass dome. Fair warning: fold the batter one stroke too many and the whole batch is ruined — which is why even Parisians buy theirs.
exp-05: Fragonard is a perfume house from Grasse, the Provençal village that has been the perfume capital of the world since the 1600s — back when its glove-makers started scenting leather for queens. Their museum sits in a gorgeous Napoleon III townhouse near the Opéra, and inside you'll play 'nose' for a day, blending your own fragrance the way master perfumers do. Professional noses train for years to distinguish thousands of scents — and it takes about a ton of petals to distill one precious liter of rose essence.
exp-06: The Moulin Rouge opened in 1889, the very same year as the Eiffel Tower, and scandalized Paris with the cancan — a dance so rebellious its high kicks were considered downright indecent. This is where Toulouse-Lautrec sat sketching the star dancer La Goulue, creating posters that became some of the most famous artworks of the Belle Époque. The red windmill on the roof is a wink to the real windmills that once dotted Montmartre — and the dancers still perform the French Cancan every single night.
exp-07: Crazy Horse was founded in 1951 by Alain Bernardin, an avant-garde art lover who dreamed of turning the female form into a living canvas — here the dancers perform almost entirely painted in projected light, an effect decades ahead of its time. The performers all take poetic stage names and are famously selected to nearly identical measurements, so the choreography reads as one flowing artwork. Beyoncé filmed here and credits the Crazy Horse as inspiration for her 'Partition' video.
exp-08: You're descending into a genuine medieval cellar — these vaulted stone rooms are whispered to have hosted secret societies and revolutionaries long before the music arrived. In 1946, just after the Liberation, jazz moved in, making this one of the first places in Europe where you could hear bebop and swing the night away underground. If it looks familiar — Ryan Gosling and Emma Stone came here in La La Land. And unlike most jazz clubs, this one expects you to dance.
exp-09: There's no more cinematic way to see Paris than from a vintage sidecar, wind in your hair, weaving past monuments the way couples did in the films of the 1960s. These lovingly restored motorcycles are throwbacks to when the sidecar was the family car of France, and your driver doubles as a storyteller who knows corners no bus can reach. Locals smile and wave at you constantly — for one golden hour, you're the main character in a French New Wave movie.
exp-10: Those dark green boxes belong to the bouquinistes, booksellers who have hawked books on these quays since the 1500s — they were once blamed for spreading forbidden ideas, and some say their pamphlets helped stoke the Revolution. Today around two hundred sellers run nearly a thousand boxes along three kilometers, which is why Parisians say the Seine is 'the only river in the world that flows between two bookshelves.' By rule, the boxes must sell mostly old books — not souvenirs.
exp-11: Sacré-Cœur crowns the highest natural point in Paris, and at sunrise you'll have its famous steps almost to yourself — a small miracle. The basilica took nearly forty years to build and has a secret superpower: its travertine stone releases a chalky white substance when it rains, so the church washes itself whiter with every storm. Up in the bell tower hangs the Savoyarde, one of the heaviest bells in the world at over eighteen tons — and inside, a prayer vigil has continued uninterrupted for more than a century.
exp-12: Le Perchoir — 'the perch' — opened in 2013 atop an unassuming building in Ménilmontant and basically invented the Parisian rooftop scene, in a city where drinking above the seventh floor was practically unheard of. Ménilmontant itself was once a hilltop village of vineyards and cabarets outside the city walls, where Parisians came to drink untaxed wine — so sipping up here at golden hour is a very old tradition in new clothes. The view is the real cocktail: all of Paris spilling toward the Eiffel Tower.
exp-13: Every summer, the lawns of Parc de la Villette become one of Europe's biggest free open-air cinemas, with thousands of Parisians sprawled on the grass before a giant inflatable screen. The park has a gritty past — this was the site of the city's slaughterhouses and cattle market until the 1970s, before being reborn as a futuristic park dotted with bright-red architectural follies. Rent a deck chair, bring a picnic and a bottle, and you're doing summer exactly like a local.
exp-14: Guinguettes were the riverside dance halls where working-class Parisians escaped on Sundays to drink cheap wine, eat fried fish, and waltz under the trees — the name comes from 'guinguet,' the tart local wine served outside the city's tax walls. Renoir and the Impressionists immortalized this world of straw hats and rowing boats, and on the Marne the tradition never died. Come on a Sunday afternoon and you'll see grandmothers, hipsters, and total strangers dancing musette to an accordion — the happiest time machine in France.
exp-15: The Palais Garnier is so extravagant it has its own underground water tank — the mysterious 'lake' that inspired The Phantom of the Opera, which Paris firefighters really do use for training. The phantom legend got a boost from a true event: in 1896, a counterweight from the grand chandelier crashed down into the audience. Look up during the performance — that dreamlike ceiling swirling with lovers and Paris landmarks was painted by Marc Chagall in 1964, a riot of modern color inside a temple of gold and velvet.
exp-16: When Père Lachaise opened in 1804 it was considered too far from town, so Paris pulled off history's greatest marketing stunt: they transferred the remains of Molière and the legendary lovers Héloïse and Abélard here, and suddenly everyone wanted in. Today over a million people rest on this hillside — Chopin, though his heart was smuggled back to Warsaw in a jar; Oscar Wilde, whose tomb gathered so many lipstick kisses it now has a glass barrier; Édith Piaf; and Jim Morrison, whose grave needed its own guard.
exp-17: For the first time in a century, you can legally swim in the Seine — public bathing was banned back in 1923, and it took a 1.4-billion-euro cleanup ahead of the 2024 Olympics to bring the river back to life. The Bercy site opened in the summer of 2025, fulfilling a promise famously made decades ago by Mayor Jacques Chirac, who vowed he'd swim in the Seine himself. When you slip into the water, you're doing something your great-grandparents' generation was the last to enjoy.
exp-18: The market at Place Maubert is one of the oldest market sites in Paris — this square has hosted traders since the Middle Ages, when it was also, less charmingly, a spot for public executions. Today you'll shop it like a Parisian chef: choosing cheese by smell, learning why the fishmonger and not the recipe decides tonight's menu, then cooking a real French meal. It's the most delicious lesson in how Parisians actually eat — seasonally, locally, and with strong opinions about everything.
music-01: The Philharmonie, designed by Jean Nouvel and opened in 2015, looks like a shimmering aluminum hill covered in thousands of interlocking metal birds. Its great hall is an acoustic marvel — the audience wraps around the orchestra on floating balconies, so no seat is more than about 32 meters from the conductor. The opening had perfect Parisian drama: Nouvel boycotted his own inauguration, claiming the building wasn't finished — but the sound inside is now ranked among the finest on Earth.
music-02: The Duc des Lombards sits on rue des Lombards, a tiny medieval street that has improbably become the jazz capital of Paris, with three legendary clubs within a hundred meters of each other. Since the mid-1980s, this intimate room has hosted giants of the genre at tables so close to the stage you can hear the bassist breathe. It's the anti-stadium: about a hundred seats, two sets a night, where world-famous musicians come to play the music they actually love.
music-03: New Morning opened in 1981 in a converted printing works, and within months it became the Paris home of jazz royalty — Chet Baker played here so often in his final years that the club feels like part of his legend. Then there's Prince, who adored the room and famously showed up to play marathon surprise after-shows, jamming into the small hours. It's still gloriously unglamorous — because here, the only thing that matters is what's coming off the stage.
music-04: Sunset opened in its vaulted cellar in 1983, and the family later added Sunside on the ground floor — two clubs in one, electric jazz downstairs and acoustic upstairs. Just about every great name in modern jazz has squeezed onto these tiny stages over four decades, often testing new material on one of the most knowledgeable crowds in Europe. Paris, remember, embraced jazz when America's segregation pushed its own musicians away — rooms like this are where that love affair still burns.
music-05: L'Olympia, with its glowing red letters, is the oldest music hall in Paris — opened in 1893 by Joseph Oller, the same showman who co-founded the Moulin Rouge. This stage made Édith Piaf, hosted Jacques Brel's most famous farewells, and welcomed the Beatles for a legendary 1964 residency. The astonishing part: when the building was threatened with demolition in the 1990s, it was rebuilt just meters away as a near-exact replica — Paris simply refused to let this room die.
music-06: La Cigale — 'the cicada' — opened in 1887 at the foot of Montmartre, when Pigalle was the beating heart of Parisian nightlife, and stars like Mistinguett and Maurice Chevalier lit up its stage. After decades of decline, even a stint as a B-movie cinema, it was reborn in 1987 with a renovation by designer Philippe Starck. Now it's one of the city's most beloved venues — small enough to feel intimate, grand enough that every band on the way up dreams of playing it.
music-07: Le Trianon is a Belle Époque jewel from 1894, tucked beside the Élysée Montmartre — its gilded theatre with marble staircase and Italian-style balconies survived fires, wars, and changing fashions virtually intact. In its heyday this neighborhood was the stomping ground of Toulouse-Lautrec, and the venue has lived a dozen lives: operetta house, cinema, and now one of the dreamiest concert halls in Paris. Seeing a modern act under those nineteenth-century ceilings is pure Paris.
music-08: Sainte-Chapelle was built in the 1240s by King Louis IX — Saint Louis himself — as a giant reliquary for the Crown of Thorns, and it went up in barely seven years, lightning speed for the Middle Ages. Its fifteen soaring windows hold over a thousand stained-glass panels telling the entire biblical story, with so much glass and so little stone that the chapel seems made of pure light. Hearing Vivaldi by candlelight in here, the walls glow like the inside of a jewel box — exactly as a medieval king intended.
music-09: La Madeleine looks like a Greek temple because it nearly was one — Napoleon ordered it built as a Temple of Glory for his army before it was finally consecrated as a church. This is where Chopin's funeral was held in 1849, with Mozart's Requiem sung as he had requested — though officials hesitated, since women's voices weren't normally allowed in the choir. The great organ was played for twenty years by Camille Saint-Saëns, whom Liszt called the finest organist in the world.
music-10: Supersonic is the beating heart of the Bastille rock scene — a club with a beautifully simple promise: live indie and rock bands, almost every night, completely free. Housed near the site where the city's revolutionary spirit was born, it champions unsigned bands and hosts all-vinyl rock'n'roll dance parties until dawn. The joke among Parisians: the cover charge is zero, but the bragging rights later are priceless.
wksp-01: You're about to make chocolate in a museum that traces four thousand years of cacao, from Maya and Aztec rituals where beans were literally money, to a collector's treasure trove of a thousand objects. Chocolate reached France in 1615, tucked into the luggage of Anne of Austria when she arrived to marry Louis XIII — a royal addiction was born. Today you'll temper and mold your own bars, the way master chocolatiers have for centuries.
wksp-02: You're descending into vaulted stone cellars dug in the 18th century for the royal sommelier of Louis XV — the man who kept the king's wine, just steps from the Louvre palace. Down here in the cool dark, you'll play winemaker yourself: tasting, blending your own personal cuvée, and bottling it with a custom label. It's the rare souvenir that was literally made by you, in cellars that once served a king.
wksp-03: Beneath this cheese shop lies a 17th-century vaulted cellar where wheels of cheese quietly ripen, just as they did centuries ago. France claims well over a thousand varieties — General de Gaulle famously despaired of governing a country with so many cheeses. Down here, master fromagers walk you through tasting and pairing them with wine, and you'll understand why the French treat cheese as a national art form.
wksp-04: You're baking in the Latin Quarter on rue du Cardinal Lemoine — the very street where a young Hemingway lived when Paris was 'a moveable feast.' The baguette you're about to shape is so sacred that a 1993 French law decrees a true 'tradition' may contain only flour, water, salt, and yeast — nothing else. In 2022, UNESCO added baguette craftsmanship to humanity's cultural heritage; France bakes some six billion a year, and today one of them is yours.
wksp-05: Tucked at the end of a quiet courtyard in Ménilmontant, this little studio sits in what was once a hilltop village of vineyards and open-air dance halls — Édith Piaf grew up singing on these streets. Ceramicist Lucie Eleme keeps her wheel classes tiny, just a few people, so the clay gets your full attention. There's something fitting about shaping earth by hand in a neighborhood that's always made beautiful things from humble beginnings.
wksp-06: Here's a secret of the Paris flower trade: the vast majority of blooms sold in France are imported, flown in from Dutch auctions and as far as Kenya. Désirée is part of the rebellion — a 'slow flower' shop championing seasonal blooms grown in French soil, often cut just hours away. You'll learn to build a bouquet the Parisian way: loose, asymmetric, and a little wild, like the gardens the flowers came from.
wksp-07: Montmartre still hides what the crowds on the basilica steps never find: secret gardens behind the lanes, and even a working vineyard, harvested with a festival every October. Renoir himself painted in a garden on this hill, in the grounds of what's now the Musée de Montmartre. Settling in with watercolors among the greenery, you're doing exactly what drew Renoir, Utrillo, and Picasso up here — chasing that soft Montmartre light.
wksp-08: At Ponoie, you'll set real dried flowers into plant-based wax — every candle is vegan, and no two ever come out alike. You're also joining a very old Parisian craft: the city's master wax-makers once supplied Versailles, and Cire Trudon, founded in 1643, lit the court of Louis XIV. Royal candlelight to flower-strewn keepsake — the tradition continues with your own two hands.
wksp-09: Since 1998, an anonymous artist called Invader has cemented more than 1,500 little tiled space invaders onto Paris walls — over 4,000 worldwide — and devotees hunt them with a special app like a citywide treasure hunt. Each one is classic mosaic technique, the same craft as a Roman floor, applied to 8-bit video game aliens. In this workshop you'll cut and set tiles to build your own pixel creature — street art you're actually allowed to take home.
wksp-10: Your teacher, chef Stéphane, trained at a famous crêpe school in Brittany before opening this crêperie, and he's been initiating Parisians into the art ever since. You'll master both kinds: lacy sweet crêpes and savory galettes made from sarrasin — buckwheat, naturally gluten-free, which legend says crusaders carried back from the East. Remember the French tradition: flip a crêpe while holding a coin in your other hand, and the year ahead brings prosperity.
wksp-11: The school's name is a wink to history: Lutèce — Lutetia — was the Roman name of Paris two thousand years ago, when it was a small town on an island in the Seine. The language you're about to wrestle with has been fiercely guarded by the Académie française since 1635, and for roughly three centuries after 1066, French was the language of the English royal court. So when English feels close to French — it is. About a third of English vocabulary came from here.
wksp-12: The riverbanks you'll be photographing are themselves a UNESCO World Heritage site — and fittingly, Paris is where photography was born. In 1838, Louis Daguerre aimed his lens at a Paris boulevard and captured the first human being ever photographed: a man standing still to have his shoes shined. Tonight you're working with the same materials as Monet and the great street photographers — the Seine, its bridges, and that famous silvery Paris light.
photo-01: You're standing on a two-story bridge from 1905 — cars and walkers down here, the Métro rumbling over your head on that steel viaduct. It was renamed in 1948 for Bir Hakeim, the desert battle where outnumbered Free French troops held off Rommel's tanks for sixteen days. And if these arcades feel familiar, that's because Christopher Nolan filmed the famous dream-bending mirror scene from Inception right here.
photo-02: This tiny, hushed avenue is named for Luís de Camões, Portugal's greatest poet — and it hides one of the most perfectly framed Eiffel Tower views in Paris. The elegant stone staircase and its curling wrought-iron balustrade have made it a darling of fashion shoots and film crews for decades. The trick locals know: the street is so short and quiet that you often get the view, and the tower, entirely to yourself.
photo-03: Rue de l'Université runs through what were once meadows owned by the medieval University of Paris — hence the name — and it's lined with the private mansions of the old aristocracy. From this spot, the Eiffel Tower fills the end of the street like a stage set. When it went up in 1889, three hundred artists signed a furious protest calling it a monstrosity — and legend says Guy de Maupassant lunched in the tower's restaurant because it was the only place in Paris he couldn't see it.
photo-04: This little fountain has been pouring since 1806, built under Napoleon to supply water to soldiers from the military hospital nearby. Look at the relief: that's Hygeia, goddess of health, tending to Mars, god of war — medicine soothing the soldier. The beloved bistro wrapped around it has been here for over a century, and in 2009 the Obamas slipped in for dinner, instantly making its red-checked tablecloths world famous.
photo-05: Often called the most beautiful bridge in Paris, this was a diplomatic love letter: Tsar Nicholas II laid the first stone in 1896, naming it for his father to seal the Franco-Russian alliance. Built for the 1900 World's Fair, its engineers pulled off a daring single steel arch just six meters high — strict orders, so it wouldn't block the view from the Champs-Élysées to Les Invalides. Those gilded winged horses? They're Fames, holding the bridge down like jewelry.
photo-06: You're standing on the roof of a store that began in 1894 as a tiny haberdashery run by two cousins. Right below your feet is the famous 1912 neo-Byzantine stained-glass dome, soaring 43 meters above the perfume counters. And here's the wild part: in 1919, daredevil pilot Jules Védrines actually landed his biplane on this rooftop to win a 25,000-franc bet — he collected the prize, and a fine from the police.
photo-07: Printemps opened in 1865 and loved being first: one of the first Paris stores with fixed price tags, and among the first lit by electricity, which crowds came just to gawk at. Its jewel is the Art Nouveau stained-glass cupola of over three thousand panes, crowning the brasserie since 1923. When war loomed in 1939 the whole thing was taken apart pane by pane and hidden — then rebuilt decades later from the original plans, by the grandson of the master glassmaker who made it.
photo-08: This little square is a shrine to Jules Lavirotte, the most flamboyant Art Nouveau architect Paris ever produced — his swirling ceramic facades here and at 29 Avenue Rapp won the city's official facade competition in 1901. The glazed sandstone came from Alexandre Bigot, the era's star ceramicist, and the suggestive carvings scandalized polite society. Stand in just the right spot by the gate and you get the bonus: the Eiffel Tower photobombing the architecture.
photo-09: This cobbled lane is one of the oldest paths in Montmartre — 'abreuvoir' means drinking trough, and farmers once led their cows down here to water them when this was still a village of windmills and vineyards. The little pink café, La Maison Rose, was opened by Germaine Pichot, a dancer from Picasso's circle — the same Germaine whose rejection drove his friend Casagemas to suicide, the tragedy that plunged Picasso into his Blue Period. Utrillo painted the house, and his canvas made it immortal.
photo-10: That bronze bust is Dalida — born Iolanda Gigliotti in Cairo, crowned Miss Egypt, and then one of the best-selling singers in history with well over 100 million records. She lived just up the street for twenty-five years, until her death in 1987, and Montmartre adored her so much they gave her this square ten years later. Notice the shine on the statue's chest — visitors rub it for luck, and the bronze has been polished to gold.
photo-11: When I.M. Pei's glass pyramid was unveiled in 1989, Parisians were furious — a modernist diamond in the courtyard of a royal palace! Now it's as beloved as the tower it once scandalized. The Louvre itself began as a medieval fortress in 1190, and at night the pyramid glows above its buried walls. One delicious myth refuses to die: that it contains exactly 666 panes of glass, the devil's number — even The Da Vinci Code repeated it. The real count is 673.
photo-12: These 260 striped marble columns landed in the courtyard of Richelieu's 17th-century palace in 1986, and the outrage was so fierce there were lawsuits to stop construction mid-build. Artist Daniel Buren got the last laugh: it's now one of the most photographed artworks in Paris. Look down through the grates — the columns continue underground — and join the tradition of tossing a coin onto one for luck.
photo-13: This was the first metal bridge in Paris, ordered by Napoleon in 1804 as an iron garden promenade across the Seine — the version you're on was rebuilt in the 1980s after barges battered the original. You may know it as the love-locks bridge: by 2014 a million padlocks weighed about 45 tons, until a railing actually collapsed and the city cut them all off. The golden dome facing you is the Institut de France, guardian of the French language since 1635.
photo-14: Yes, that's really her — a quarter-scale Statue of Liberty, given by the American community of Paris in 1889 to thank France for her big sister in New York Harbor. Her tablet carries two dates: July 4, 1776 and July 14, 1789 — twin revolutions. She originally faced east toward the Élysée Palace, but in 1937 she was turned around to gaze west, directly toward New York, across four thousand miles of ocean.
photo-15: These grand terraces and fountains by André Le Nôtre — the gardener of Versailles — once framed a magnificent château where history kept happening: Napoleon staged the 1799 coup that brought him to power here. Then in 1870, with Prussian troops occupying it, the château caught fire — struck, in cruel irony, by a French shell — and the ruins were razed. Today you stroll through a masterpiece garden whose palace is a ghost, with one of the finest panoramas over Paris.
photo-16: This sparkling carousel spins on what was, for centuries, the darkest square in Paris: the Place de Grève, where public executions drew huge crowds. It's also where day laborers gathered hoping for work — which is why 'faire grève' became the French phrase for going on strike. And the magnificent city hall behind it is actually a replica: Communards burned the original to the ground in 1871, and Paris rebuilt it even grander.
photo-17: Here's the most charming lie in Montmartre: that apartment building isn't sinking at all. Stand on the steep grassy slope beside Sacré-Cœur, tilt your camera so the hill looks level, and suddenly the perfectly ordinary building behind it appears to be sliding into the earth. It's pure optical illusion — real people live in there, completely level — and Instagram turned this sneaky camera trick into one of the most famous photos in Paris.
photo-18: This graceful green footbridge was thrown up as a temporary walkway for the 1900 World's Fair — and like the Eiffel Tower, it simply never left. It's named for General Debilly, a Napoleonic officer killed at the Battle of Jena in 1806. But its best story is from the Cold War: the bridge earned a reputation as a discreet rendezvous for East German agents, and spy lore has clung to it ever since.
